content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
July 19, 199 0
Honorable Bobbie llccowan
97 01 Las Tunas Drive
P. O. Box 668
Temple city, 
cA
Dear Ms. llccowan:
This letter is in response to your request for advice reqard-
ing your duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-
interest and disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Acttt).1
OUESTIONS
1. You are an elected member of the Temple ci'ty city
Councit. Does your position as an elected mernber of the
California Teachers Association governing body create a conflict
of interest under the Act?
2. Does reimbursement for travel expenses, lodging and meals
constitute reportable income for purposes of the Act?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Your elected position to the california Teachers As-
sociation governing body does not create a conflict of interest
under the Act.
2. Reimbursernent for travel expenses, lodgj-ng and meals does
not constitute reportabl-e income to the extent the Cali'fornia
Teachers Association is a tax exempt corporation organized
exclusively for educational, academic, and charitable purposes and
you providi the association with adequate consideration for any
payments you receive.
1 Gor".rr*"nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A11 statutory references
are to tlre Government code unless otherwise indicated. comnission
iefu:.ations appear at 2 Catifornia Code of Regulations. Section
isooo, et seo-'- A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
pivisioil-sE the california Code of Regulations'
9■780-0668
Re: Your Request for Advice
. our File No. A-90-452
428JStreet,suite8o0 o P.o.Box8o7 o sacramentocA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322'5660


Fi■e No. A-90-452
Page 2
IACIS
You have been a teacher in the state of cal-ifornia and a
member of the california Teachers Association (c.T.A.) for 29
years. fn April 1990 you were elected to the city council of the
City of Tenp1e city. 
You are an unsalarj.ed elected menber of the
governingT body of the C.T.A., State council.
As an elected mernber of the governing body of the c.T.A. you
must attend eight rneetings per year to act on c.T.A. policy nat-
ters. 
You are reirnbursed for travel expenses, lodging and meals.
To receive rej-mbursement you must attend all rneetings and sign
attendance sheets. You nust also present an itenj,zed bill- and
recej.pts. Depending on the cost of air fare, reimbursernents total
approximately $900 per year.
You are also an elected representative of the c.T.A. before
the National Education Association (N.E.A.) Resol-utions Assenbly
to be held in Kansas City, Missouri. The c,T.A. will nake a pay-
rnent of $875 to each state representative to this assembly to
cover the cost of travel, lodging and meals. To receive this
funding you must attend atl rneetings and sign daj-ly attendance
sheets. Unused funds nust be returned to the c.T.A. Entertain-
ment is not included in this funding. While in Kansas city you
wifl vote on N.E.A. proposed amendments to its constitution,
bylaws, and standing rules, and you will also participate in the
selection of new N.E.A. officers.
ANALYSIS
The Political 
Reforrn Act does not prohibit a public official
from participating in nongovernmental activities. 
Ho$/ever, the
conflict-of- interest provisions of the Act may at tj'rnes requj-re
that a public official 
disqualify himsel'f or herself from
participating in certain decisions.
Section 8710o prohibits any public official 
frorn rnaking,
participating in rnaking, or otherwise using his or- l.ter official-
position to influence i governmental decision in which the of-
ii"i"f 
has a financial interest. 
As a menber of the city council
"i tt" city of Temple City you are a public official' 
(Section
a2o4a. ) 
_
An official 
has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonabty foreseeable . that the
decisi5n sril,1 have a materj,al financial effect, distinguishable
frorn its effect on the public generallyr on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a) Any busl'ness entity in which the pubLic
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more'


File No. A-90-452
Page 3
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand doll-ars (S1,00O) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating thro hundred fifty 
doJ,-
lars ($25o) or more in value provided to, received
by or prornised to the public off icj,a1 within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or hofds any position of rnanagement.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars (5250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or pro:nised to the public
official 
within 12 nonths prior to the tine when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section, indj.rect invest-
nent or interest neans any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
offi,ciat, by an agent on behalf of a public of-
ficial, 
or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, 
the offj-cia1's agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, 
or
beneficialLy a 10-percent interest or greater.
Section 87103.
Sectj-on 87103 requires your disqua 1if ication fron participat-
ing in any governmental degision if the decision will have a
foieseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable from
the effect on the pubtic generally, on any source of income of
$250 or rnore in the 12 months preceding the decision. You have
stated in your request for advice that you exPect to receive
payments from the c.T.A. in excess of $250 during the current
ii'scat year. Participation in c.T.A. activities does not in and
of itself create a conflict of interest under the Act' 
However,
if the pa)mrents you recej.ve from the c.T.A, are rePortable incorne
to you, tien you-must not participate in governrnental decisions
\"/hiah 1"/i11 have a foreseeable and material financial effect on
C.i.A, v/hich is distinguishable frorn the effects on the public
generalLy.
TheActimposescertainfi].inganddisclosureobligationson
publi- officials. 
(Section 87200. i 
Sources of incone in excess


File No. A-9o-452
Page 4
of $250, and sources of gifts in excess of $50, must be disclosed
in the official's 
yearly statement of economic interests.
(section 872o7. ) Income incl-udes gi,fts, j-ncluding any gift of
food or beverage, reimbursement for expenses, and per diem,
(section 8203o (a) . )
Thus, both incorne and gifts must be disclosed, and both
income and gifts of 5250 or more are potentially disquali,fying
financial interests. 
Under the facts presented in your letter,
the characterization of the payments received from C.T.A. is of
irnportance. If the payments are characterized as income, the pay-
ments nay fa11 within one of the specified exceptions to the
definition of rrincome" and, thus, not be reportable. 
(Section
82030(b) (2).) 
If the payments are gifts, the exceptions to incorne
do not apply and the pa)rments are discLosable, (Rj,ddle Advice
Letter, No. A-89-200, copy enclosed.)
The prirnary distinction between a gift and income is whether
the recipient has provided equal consideration in exchange for the
payment. where a payment is received and the recipient does not
provide consideration of equal or greater value to the source of
the paynent, the payment is considered a gift. 
conversely, where
the recipient can denonstrate that he or she has provided equal or
greater consideration for the payment, the pa)rment is treated as
income. (Torrance Advice Letter, No. A-89-648, copy enclosed')
Equal consideration means that each party receives benefits
of equal vaIue. (fn re Burciaqa (L975) 2 FPPC ops. 17, copy
enclosed.) while we have no exact formula to determine whether
consideration of equal or greater value has been provided by the
official, 
the follorring general guidelines may be of assistance.
The value of services rendered may be proven by evidence as to the
customary rate of compensation for such services, irrespective of
official 
status. 
(Tassi v Tassi (1958) 160 cal.App.2d 680, 690-
691.) Also relevant in the determination rnight be the length of
time spent rendering the services, . or whether the services are of
the type not readily available from others. U1tinately, however,
the dltermination oi whether equal consideration has been provided
is necessaril-y a factual- one. (Ibid.)
You are a teacher, an elected member of the california Teach-
ers Association, and one of its representatives before the
NationaL Education AssoLiation. In order to discharge your duties
you travel to meetj-ngs. while at the meetings, you participate in
&iscussions and vote on natters affecting the membership of the
California Teachers Associ-ation and the National Education As-
sociation. 
Your attendance at these meetingTs is recorded in the
daj-1y attendance sheet. You, as well as all other delegates, are
reirnlursed for travel dxpenses, lodging, and rneals' Under the
circumstances, $/e conclude that the paynents you receive are
income. Your active participation in the nanagement' of both
organizations constitutes consideration of equal or greater value
for the payments you receive from c.T'A'


Fil,e No. A-9o-452
Page 5
We nor4r determine whether this income is disclosable. 
The
statutory scheme excludes from the definition of 'rincome'r any
reimbursernent for travel- expenses and per diem received from a
bona fide educational, academic, or charj.table organization.
(Section 82030(b) (2).) 
A corporation which is organj-zed and oper-
ated exclusively for educational, academic. or charitable
purposes, and which is exempt frorn taxation under the provisions
of section 501(c) (3) of the Internal Revenue code qualif j.es for
this limited exception to the reporting and disclosure require-
ments of the Act, 
(Sonoda Advice Letter, No. A-89-179, copy
encl,osed. ) 
If you determine that the California Teachers As-
sociation is, in fact, a tax exempt educational, academic, or
charitable organization, then any reimbursement of travel and per
diem you receive from the organization j-s exc]uded from the
definition of j.ncorne and is nej.ther reportabJ.e as income nor
disqualifying under the conf 1j,ct-of- interest provisions of the Act
to the extent that you have provided the organization with
adequate consideration.
We trust this letter is responsive to your inquiry. 
should
you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not
hesitate to call me at (916) 322-59or.
S incere 1y ,
Scott Hallabri,n
Acting ceneral counsel-
SH:BMB:plh
Enclosures
α疵ユRち。
By: Blanca M.
counsel,
Breez e
Legal Division
